5.text: The key insight: instead of improving one model's outputs, we improve the model population itself through evolutionary pressure.

6.text: Each variant has different hyperparameters -- this diversity is what gives evolution something to select from.

9.text: PBT is the core algorithm here: train many models in parallel, periodically replacing the worst with mutated copies of the best.

22.text: No LLM needed for mutations -- simple random perturbations of numeric hyperparameters are enough for effective search.

24.text: Validation loss is our fitness function -- evolution will minimize it by discovering good hyperparameter combinations.

37.text: Fixed seeds ensure reproducibility -- every run produces the same evolutionary trajectory for fair comparison.

51.text: Shuffling before the train/val split prevents any ordering bias (e.g. alphabetical) from skewing results.

62.text: Holding out 1000 names for validation is critical -- fitness must be measured on unseen data to avoid overfitting.

69.text: block_size is fixed across the population -- it defines the maximum context window all models share.

120.text: MicroGPT accepts n_embd, n_head, and n_layer as constructor arguments, making the architecture evolvable.

125.text: n_layer controls depth -- evolution can discover whether 1 or 2 layers works best for this task.

126.text: The lm_head projects back to vocab_size, which stays fixed regardless of the evolved embedding dimension.

132.text: Small init std (0.08) keeps initial outputs low-entropy -- important so all population members start trainable.

136.text: Token and position embeddings are summed then normalized -- this is the model's input pipeline.

147.text: HP_SPACE defines the search space -- evolution can only explore combinations of these discrete values.

148.text: Embedding dimensions range from tiny (16) to small (64) -- the population will discover which size works best.

149.text: Number of attention heads: 1, 2, or 4. Must evenly divide n_embd, which the code enforces.

150.text: Depth is limited to 1 or 2 layers -- even this binary choice has a big impact on model capacity vs. training speed.

151.text: Learning rate varies by 4x across the space -- this is often the most impactful hyperparameter to tune.

152.text: Beta1 controls momentum in Adam -- lower values make the optimizer more responsive to recent gradients.

156.text: random_config() is the initialization operator: it creates a single random individual for the population.

158.text: Each hyperparameter is sampled independently and uniformly from its allowed values.

160.text: Constraint repair: if n_embd isn't divisible by n_head, resample n_head until the constraint is satisfied.

165.text: mutate_config() is the mutation operator -- the 'explore' step that introduces variation into the population.

167.text: Copy first, then mutate -- we never modify the parent's config, preserving it for potential reuse.

168.text: Usually mutate 1 hyperparameter, sometimes 2. Small mutations keep children close to proven parents.

169.text: random.sample picks which hyperparameters to change -- any combination is possible.

171.text: The new value is sampled uniformly from the full range, not perturbed from the current value. This is a 'resample' mutation.

173.text: Same constraint repair as in random_config -- mutations must also produce valid architectures.

178.text: build_model() is a factory: given a config dict, it returns a fresh MicroGPT with those hyperparameters.

180.text: The config dict is unpacked into constructor arguments -- this decouples evolution logic from model internals.

183.text: train_model() runs a fixed number of gradient steps -- in PBT, all members train for the same number of steps per generation.

185.text: The optimizer uses the evolved lr and beta1 -- these are the 'phenotype' that evolution tunes.

186.text: beta1 comes from the evolved config, beta2 is fixed at 0.99. Evolution only searches over the most impactful knob.

189.text: Cycling through train_data with modulo ensures we use all examples even if num_steps exceeds dataset size.

196.text: Cross-entropy loss is the training signal -- each member independently minimizes its own loss.

201.text: Returning average loss lets us monitor training quality, though fitness is measured separately on validation data.

204.text: @torch.no_grad() disables gradient tracking during evaluation -- we only need forward passes for fitness.

205.text: evaluate_model() computes the fitness function: validation loss on held-out data. Lower is better.

209.text: Capping at max_samples=200 speeds up evaluation -- we don't need the full val set to rank members.

220.text: Average validation loss is the fitness score. The evolution loop will sort the population by this value.

224.text: generate_names() lets us qualitatively inspect what each model has learned -- a sanity check beyond loss numbers.

230.text: Autoregressive generation: predict one token at a time, append it, and feed the growing sequence back in.

234.text: Temperature controls randomness: 0.5 is fairly conservative, producing more typical (less surprising) names.

235.text: BOS token acts as both start and end marker -- generating BOS means the name is complete.

250.text: Phase 1 establishes a baseline: one randomly configured model trained for 500 steps. Evolution must beat this.

253.text: Counting parameters shows model capacity -- evolution might discover that smaller or larger models work better.

256.text: 500 training steps for the baseline gives it a fair amount of learning before we compare.

257.text: baseline_val_loss is the number to beat -- if evolution can't improve on a random config, it isn't working.

268.text: POP_SIZE=8 is small but enough to demonstrate selection pressure. Production PBT uses dozens to hundreds.

269.text: NUM_GENERATIONS=6 means 6 rounds of train-evaluate-select-mutate. Each generation refines the population.

270.text: STEPS_PER_GEN=200 means each member trains for 200 steps before being evaluated. Total: 200 x 6 = 1200 steps for survivors.

271.text: TOP_K=3 means the best 3 survive each generation. The other 5 are replaced by mutated offspring.

275.text: Population initialization: create POP_SIZE individuals, each with a random config and fresh model.

278.text: val_loss starts at infinity -- every member must earn its fitness through actual training and evaluation.

283.text: Selection pressure: top-3 survive, bottom-5 are replaced. This is truncation selection, the simplest form.

287.text: The main evolution loop: each generation trains, evaluates, selects, and mutates the population.

289.text: Inner loop: every population member trains for the same number of steps -- fair comparison.

290.text: Training continues from where the member left off -- survivors accumulate learning across generations.

291.text: After training, measure fitness. This val_loss determines who survives and who gets replaced.

294.text: Sorting by val_loss implements fitness ranking -- population[0] is the fittest, population[-1] is the weakest.

296.text: Track the best individual each generation -- this lets us see if evolution is making progress.

298.text: Average loss across the population shows overall population fitness, not just the best individual.

320.text: Survivors are the elite: the top-K members that pass to the next generation unchanged.

321.text: Survivors keep their trained weights -- this is the 'exploit' part of PBT. Good solutions are preserved.

323.text: We need POP_SIZE - TOP_K new children to fill the population back to its original size.

325.text: Random parent selection from survivors: each child descends from one of the top-K. This spreads good genes.

326.text: mutate_config creates the child's config by randomly perturbing 1-2 hyperparameters of the parent.

329.text: Architecture change detection: if n_embd, n_head, or n_layer changed, the weight tensors have different shapes.

335.text: If architecture changed, the child starts from scratch -- weight inheritance is impossible with different tensor shapes.

336.text: A fresh model with random weights. This child must learn everything from zero, but may have a better architecture.

338.text: The PBT 'exploit' step: same architecture means we can copy the parent's learned weights to the child.

340.text: load_state_dict copies all parameters -- the child starts where the parent left off, a huge head start.

342.text: Each child gets a unique ID for tracking lineage across generations.

345.text: New children start with inf val_loss -- they haven't been evaluated yet and will be assessed next generation.

349.text: Replace the entire population with survivors + children. The cycle repeats.

359.text: Final sort to identify the overall best model after all generations of evolution.

360.text: best_evolved is the fittest individual discovered across the entire evolutionary run.

365.text: Generation-by-generation table shows whether evolution is making progress -- best loss should decrease over time.

373.text: The key comparison: did evolution find a better configuration than a single random guess?

375.text: Positive improvement means evolution beat the baseline -- the search found a better region of hyperparameter space.

380.text: Qualitative comparison: do the evolved model's generated names look more realistic than the baseline's?

389.text: Population diversity in the final generation: shows whether evolution converged to one config or maintained variety.

391.text: Printing each member's config reveals how much the population has converged -- similar configs suggest a fitness peak.

408.text: The PBT loop in summary: initialize, train, evaluate, select, mutate, inherit, repeat.

418.text: Lab 23 improves training data (output quality); Lab 24 improves the model architecture and optimizer settings.

420.text: Lab 23 is like self-play refinement (STaR); Lab 24 is like natural selection -- fundamentally different strategies.

424.text: EXPLOIT: children inherit trained weights from successful parents, getting a head start on learning.

425.text: EXPLORE: random hyperparameter mutations ensure the population doesn't get stuck in a local optimum.

428.text: PBT was originally designed to evolve learning rate schedules and data augmentation during training.

430.text: FunSearch uses the same pattern but evolves LLM-generated programs instead of numeric hyperparameters.

431.text: Neural Architecture Search (NAS) is the extreme version: evolve the network topology itself.
